title: Levenshtein (Edit) Distance
concept: Edit distance counts the minimum single-character edits (insertions, deletions, substitutions) needed to turn one word into another.

options.text: The minimum number of single-character edits to turn one word into another
The total number of letters in two words combined
How fast you can type a word on a keyboard

exercises.explain: Edit distance measures the minimum number of single-character operations (insert, delete, substitute) to turn one string into another.

cards.title: Oops, Typo!
cards.body: Ever wondered how search engines know you meant 'chocolate' when you accidentally type 'choclate'? They use a clever math trick called Edit Distance to find the closest word with the fewest typos!

cards.title: What is Edit Distance?
cards.body: Levenshtein Distance (also called Edit Distance) is a way to measure how different two words are. It counts the minimum number of single-character edits—insertions, deletions, or substitutions—needed to transform one word into another.

cards.title: The Toy Block Swap
cards.body: Imagine you have a row of toy blocks spelling a word. If you want to change it into a new word, you can only do three things: slide a new block in (insert), take a block away (delete), or swap one block for another (substitute). The fewer moves you make to reach the new word, the closer the words are!

cards.title: See It In Action
cards.body:
Let's transform 'cat' into 'dog':
1. Change 'c' to 'd' ('dat')
2. Change 'a' to 'o' ('dot')
3. Change 't' to 'g' ('dog')
That took 3 steps, so the edit distance is 3!
To change 'cat' to 'cats', we just insert 's', which takes 1 step (distance of 1).

cards.title: Myth: Any Path Works
cards.body: It might seem like you can only change letters one by one in a straight line, but Edit Distance always looks for the absolute shortest shortcut. It ignores longer paths to find the minimum number of moves possible.

cards.title: Visualizing Edits
cards.body: See how different operations change the word 'ten' into other words. Each path represents one edit unit.

right.text: Add 't' at the end to make 'tent' (Distance: 1)

exercises.prompt: Which of the following transformations has an Edit Distance of exactly 1?
exercises.explain: Changing 'run' to 'rain' requires adding 'a' and swapping 'u' for 'i', which takes 2 steps. Changing 'run' to 'sun' only requires swapping 'r' for 's', which is just 1 edit step.

exercises.prompt: The Edit Distance between the words 'sad' and 'ad' is 1.
exercises.explain: The distance is indeed 1 because you only need to delete the letter 's' from 'sad' to get 'ad'.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the description of edit operations.
exercises.sentence: Adding a brand new letter to a word is called an insertion.
exercises.explain: Adding a new letter that wasn't there before (like turning 'pin' into 'pine') is called an insertion.

exercises.prompt: Two AI models are analyzing the words 'ace', 'act', and 'map'. Which model correctly calculates the edit distances?

options.text: AI Model A: The distance from 'ace' to 'map' is 1, and 'ace' to 'act' is 3.
AI Model B: The distance from 'ace' to 'map' is 3, and 'ace' to 'act' is 1.
AI Model C: Both transformations have a distance of 2.

exercises.explain: AI Model B is correct because changing 'ace' to 'map' requires replacing all three letters ('a'->'m', 'c'->'a', 'e'->'p'), which takes 3 edits. Changing 'ace' to 'act' only requires 1 edit ('e'->'t').

exercises.prompt: You are building a spell-checker and need to calculate the Edit Distance between the typed word 'cats' and the dictionary word 'cot'. What is the minimum number of edits required?
exercises.explain: To change 'cats' to 'cot': delete 's' (cats -> cat), and swap 'a' for 'o' (cat -> cot). This requires exactly 2 operations. Changing 'cats' to 'cats' takes 0 edits, 'cats' to 'cat' takes 1 edit (deletion), and 'cats' to 'coat' takes 2 edits but 'cot' is the correct 2-edit match here.
